0x358E76A0C6224dCABCe39ff395b284e7da9bf4.title: netto.eth | CEO at blockful
0x358E76A0C6224dCABCe39ff395b284e7da9bf4.motivation:
I aim to make DAO governance secure. With my company, I have developed a risk assessment framework, along with tools and a monitoring system, to better understand and contribute to the ecosystem, thereby reducing the risk of attacks and governance vulnerabilities in decentralized organizations.

By joining the Security Council, I bring both a governance-first perspective and hands-on technical experience. This dual expertise positions me to support the council in safeguarding Arbitrum’s infrastructure, DAO, and community.
0x358E76A0C6224dCABCe39ff395b284e7da9bf4.experience:
Recently, I led the technical process that helped secure the governance of Shutter, a DAO whose governance was vulnerable and could have lost $3M if me and my team had not identified the issues and proposed solutions to its members. Read the full case here: https://paragraph.com/@blockful/shutter-attack-prevention

Shutter is not the first DAO where I have worked to identify vulnerabilities and propose ways to protect its governance. It all started with ENS, where I uncovered a potential attack that could have drained its entire treasury, valued at over $150M. As a result, I proposed the implementation of a Security Council at ENS to protect it from future threats: https://paragraph.com/@research.blockful/a-hidden-threat-to-ens-uncovering-and-solving-a-major-governance-risk

Building on this experience, I also led my team when we assessed the security of Uniswap governance, where blockful received a grant to develop the Anticapture framework — our DAO security product — and implement it within Uniswap: https://uniswapfoundation.mirror.xyz/SAPBIdMcJpo\_gUUyHdMNuH8r7qpCqRtxFbDrui7Na-I

Beyond these cases, I have studied vulnerabilities across other DAOs in the ecosystem, aiming to deepen the understanding of governance and contribute to a more secure market. Compound is one such example, where I led the technical exploration and later published two research pieces on attempted attacks against the organization:

* First attempt involved Humpy — a well-known DAO attacker — trying to pass a proposal to use Compound’s treasury for personal benefit: https://paragraph.com/@research.blockful/anatomy-and-antidote-for-compound-war
* Second attempt involved the Compound Foundation proposing a set of measures to protect the DAO’s governance, during which we identified Humpy moving addresses to potentially block the proposals: https://x.com/blockful_io/status/2023530187221332393

None of the attacks on Compound were successful.

Additionally, risks have been assessed and analyses published on other DAOs such as Scroll, Nouns, and Gitcoin: https://anticapture.com/

skills.cyberSecurity: Already prevented some attacks, writing and deploying smart contract that protect +$1B. I have not only code and technical capacity, but also research and mechanism design expertise.

0x358E76A0C6224dCABCe39ff395b284e7da9bf4.projects: ENS, Uniswap. I participate on Scroll governance but do not have any service provider relation, so no conflict of interest. In our treasury we hold around ~5% of L2 tokens: ARB, OP, SCR.

0xf879c33b455B69B2719c0Cc3cB06eAC77d513c.motivation:
Security Council decisions are high-stakes, often time-sensitive, and require people who actually understand what they're signing off on, technically and operationally. The combination of what SEEDGov brings as an organisation and my background as our representative is exactly what this role calls for:

- Hands-on smart contract and protocol analysis, 
- Direct experience managing multisig infrastructure, 
- Deep familiarity with Arbitrum's governance and technical stack, and 
- A track record of operating under structured, accountable frameworks.

We're not newcomers to Arbitrum, and we're not here to rubber-stamp decisions. We're here because we think governance security is just as important as protocol security, and we want to help make sure both hold up.
0xf879c33b455B69B2719c0Cc3cB06eAC77d513c.experience:
I have been in crypto for over six years, five of them full-time. I've spent two and a half years as an Associate Researcher at BCAS, and I'm currently a Blockchain and Smart Contract Advisor at Axis Group, where my day-to-day involves reviewing the code and architecture of major DeFi protocols and Layer 2s as part of Decentralisation Audits and technical assessments under MiCA. That means identifying protocol-level risks and evaluating smart contract systems under strict regulatory and security standards — I've contributed technical input to more than 40 whitepapers and several licensing processes across that time, which has given me a detailed picture of how these systems are built, how they interact, and where the failure points tend to live.

On the Arbitrum side, I'm part of the Stylus Sprint programme as a milestones reviewer, where I've reviewed more than 40 technical milestones across projects contributing core infrastructure to the Stylus stack, giving me direct exposure to Arbitrum's technical architecture and development pipeline from both a technical and governance angle. 

On the brother Layer 2 ecosystem side, I'm currently serving as Governance Facilitator for the Operations Committee of Scroll DAO, where I'm responsible for setting up and managing the DAO's multisigs. As part of that work, I've also built tooling to monitor DAO activity and multisig transactions, including a dedicated interface to track and categorise multisig operations, and Dune dashboards tracking protocol and DAO activity more broadly. I've also led technical tracks on ZK-EVM architecture through the Scroll DAO Accelerator Programme, helping onboard contributors to the practical realities of working within an L2 ecosystem.

skills.cyberSecurity: Since I’ve been involved in many DAOs and participated in many multisigs, I’ve been trained more than once in cybersecurity — for example, on Gitcoin, Scroll, etc. Beyond that, I designed the Scroll security training for multisig signers. Lastly, I’ve read many audits in my role as an Associate Researcher at BCAS, as a Blockchain and Smart Contract Advisor at Axis Group, as well as in my role as a technical milestone reviewer on the Stylus programme.

0xef7b13326c7C680735d792B63587096CF1b273.motivation:
The Security Council plays a critical role in protecting the protocol during emergency actions and governance-approved upgrades. Effective decisions require both deep familiarity with the system and strong technical judgment when evaluating security-sensitive situations.

One area I believe will become increasingly important is the use of AI-assisted security tooling and agent-based analysis to strengthen smart contract and protocol security for the Arbitrum infrastructure. Recent advances in AI systems are making it possible to augment traditional auditing techniques with automated reasoning, vulnerability discovery, and continuous monitoring. If used carefully, these approaches could significantly improve our ability to detect and prevent vulnerabilities in complex systems like Arbitrum and Stylus.

As a member of the Security Council, I would advocate for exploring and leveraging state-of-the-art security techniques, including emerging AI-based approaches, while remaining vendor-neutral and focused on open, security-first practices.

Combined with my experience auditing the protocol and working extensively with fuzzing and formal verification tools, I hope to help ensure Arbitrum continues to operate with the highest possible security standards.

0x913Af9a61d1a59aA5D21CE9Bbf7Fd44Ed61dB4.experience:
I am Pablo Sabbatella, also known as pablito.eth. I am a web3 operational security researcher, member of SEAL (Security Alliance) and I am applying for the security council as founder of Opsek. I also created “Blockchain Security series” podcast.

I started with cybersecurity back in 1999, when I founded Hackemate and have been involved in technology since then. I am part of the Optimism Security Council and the Polygon Protocol Council.

I founded Opsek, where we do operational security audits and training for Web3 organizations (DeFi, CEXs, L1s, L2s, VCs, service providers and HNWI). The reason behind my work and founding Opsek is very simple: 99% of funds being lost are due to operational security issues (Private key leakage, malware, exploits, social engineering, phishing, account takeovers, domain hijacking, etc) and not due to smart contract hacks anymore.
My expertise is understanding an organization, defining and protecting its attack surface: what does the organization do? Who is the team? What are the tools and tack that they use? What does the day to day operation look like? What are the most valuable assets it’s protecting? Which are the biggest risks? We also train the teams on physical security.
Part of our auditing process includes multisigs: how were they created? Who are the signers? What’s the appropriate threshold? How are private keys generated? How are seeds handled? Are they backed up or deleted? How do you travel with your hardware wallet? Have signers developed a threat model? Hardware wallet diversity, frontend diversity, transactions verification and simulation, definition of procedures and policies, etc.

I have already audited many firms (many of them we do not make public). Some of them: Optimism, Sky (ex MakerDao), Centrifuge, Contango, Midas, Aligned Layer, and many more.
I have participated in many war rooms, incident response and helped many people and companies save funds during attacks (and still do this daily).

0x59c8535419BbCb8AdFFDB3C835435E907e3B18.motivation:
As the Head of Strategy at the RARI Foundation, my commitment to the security and success of the Arbitrum ecosystem is both personal and professional. The RARI DAO's migration to Arbitrum places a premium on securing our assets and ensuring seamless operations for our community. Given the mission-critical nature of this migration, maintaining the integrity and security of both the RARI Chain, our Arbitrum Orbit Layer 3 solution, and the broader Arbitrum ecosystem is a top priority.
We’ve also integrated the Rarible Protocol with Arbitrum, signaling our long-term alignment with the ecosystem. As such, my motivation extends beyond safeguarding RARI's interests—it's about contributing to a secure and scalable Arbitrum infrastructure for all stakeholders. My aim is to bring both agility and responsibility to the Security Council, facilitating swift, thoughtful responses to both emergency and non-emergency scenarios.
0x59c8535419BbCb8AdFFDB3C835435E907e3B18.experience:
* Established the RARI DAO Security Council: I led the creation of the RARI DAO Security Council, which operates similarly to Arbitrum’s structure. This involved defining roles, establishing emergency protocols, and creating transparent reporting mechanisms, all while ensuring quick response capabilities in moments of vulnerability.
* Launched RARI Chain (Arbitrum Orbit L3): I led the launch of the RARI Chain as part of Arbitrum's Orbit Layer 3 solution, gaining hands-on experience with the technical and governance structures within Arbitrum’s framework.
* Multisig Experience: I’m a signor on multiple multisigs, confident in executing actions directly via Etherscan contracts when the user interface is inaccessible, ensuring operational continuity during emergencies.
* Transparent Reporting to DAO Communities: I’ve consistently reported security measures, upgrades, and governance decisions to DAO communities, prioritizing transparency and clarity. My communication strategy focuses on maintaining trust, particularly when handling sensitive security incidents.
* Crisis Management: I have managed crisis situations and led war rooms, ensuring swift resolution of security issues while balancing transparency and stakeholder trust.

0xafc7805c640C4A9E5D28f6A5Eae23b50e59B83.motivation:
At Dedaub, our fundamental mission is to enhance the security and resilience of the Web3 ecosystem. We are committed to safeguarding decentralized platforms and building trust within the community. Joining the Arbitrum Security Council represents an opportunity for us to extend our positive impact by collaborating with like-minded professionals dedicated to safeguarding a major ecosystem. Our previous initiatives, such as being a founding member of the Security Alliance (https://securityalliance.org), memberships in other security councils, and security vulnerability disclosures on 3rd party protocols such as Uniswap reflect our commitment to this cause.

A secondary motivation is that we have a number of our partners and clients who rely on the security of the Arbitrum network, which further serves to align us with this role. Therefore, we believe that by contributing our expertise to the Arbitrum Security Council, we can help fortify the platform, ensuring a safer environment for its users and stakeholders.
0xafc7805c640C4A9E5D28f6A5Eae23b50e59B83.experience:
Dedaub brings a wealth of experience in blockchain security and smart contract auditing. Our team has conducted security audits and security impact studies for the Ethereum Foundation, ensuring the Ethereum ecosystem evolves in a secure manner. Additionally, we are a member of the security council for zkSync, which presents similar challenges to being a security council member on Arbitrum. We are more than well-equipped to handle the SLA of this council.
Our experience extends to auditing important projects within the Arbitrum ecosystem, such as GMX. We have also actively participated in high-stakes bug bounty programs and emergency response scenarios. One such example is our security disclosure on the Multichain bridge, whereby we helped move $1B in various tokens that were immediately at risk. There are many other examples of ecosystem threats whereby the Dedaub team was roped in to help in a war room scenario. Experiences like this demonstrate our ability to perform under pressure and deliver effective solutions.
Being a founding member of the Security Alliance underscores our collaborative approach to industry-wide security challenges. Additionally, our role as a security advisor to the Arbitrum DAO Procurement Committee (ADPC) has allowed us to influence procurement strategies with a security-first mindset. This diverse experience equips us with the insights and skills necessary to contribute meaningfully to the Arbitrum Security Council.
Finally we bring additional jurisdiction diversity to the council by being domiciled (in both form and substance) in Malta, which further derisks the operation of the security council. We operate our own Arbitrum nodes in our datacentre, and the organization is controlled by the two founders (No VCs).

skills.cyberSecurity: We're a well-known Web3 security provider, operating since 2021. The founders have been in the web3 security space since 2018, and web2 security space since 2015. A third of our security team holds relevant CS PhDs.

For nearly four years, Code4rena has demonstrated the effectiveness of a well-harnessed security community. We created the competitive audit which has become an industry standard security practice replicated by multiple players.

Based on our experience, we see an opportunity to bring a unique presence and distinct advantage to supporting the security goals of Arbitrum.

As supporters of Arbitrum's mission to securely scale Ethereum and having participated in helping to secure Arbitrum itself through competitive audits—including [this election system](https://code4rena.com/reports/2023-08-arbitrum) and [Arbitrum BoLD](https://code4rena.com/audits/2024-05-arbitrum-bold)—we would of course be honored to continue to serve the community in new ways.

skills.cyberSecurity:
Code4rena has had a positive impact on web3 security and the ecosystem as a whole through empowering the growth and scale of security researcher expertise, transparent publication of the largest single collection of vulnerabilities and accompanying project codebases, and helping to cultivate the emergence of the independent auditor economy.

Before building Code4rena as the first competitive audit platform, Sock led the team who created the Node Security Project—the first decentralized ecosystem-wide open and collaborative audit. Data from this effort was eventually donated to the Node Foundation after being used to bootstrap both the Node Security Platform (“nsp” — a product delivering actionable security advisory intel via cli) and subsequent nsp-copycat Snyk. nsp was eventually sold to npm and is what you get when you run `npm audit`. Sock also cofounded the security audit firm which provided the first pentests for GitHub and which served as their first external security advisors.

I saw this just as I was going through my multi-signer ritual for a SAFE I'm a part of, and I thought, "Well, that would be one hell of an honor to do." Given the upcoming ApeChain launch, I'd love to get more involved with the Arbitrum ecosystem in any way I can. Still, I also felt uniquely qualified to throw my name in the hat for this responsibility.

I'm easy to reach, with geographic uniqueness and over a decade of crypto and security experience. I have extensive experience writing security policies, post-mortems, hotwash docs, and organizing fire drills, and I am more than happy to lend that skillset to the role. I love getting involved in DAOs and have been active in many of them over the years, including ApeDAO, ApeCoin DAO, Inverse Finance, BadgerDAO, and a smattering of failed projects/DAOs before forming my own (Boring Security DAO).
0x0b17eBF1d97983a925f3Acc2dC6E4Be13a24A2.experience:
Around the early 2010s, I transitioned my IT career to focus more on Security, which was my interest as a kid. Just as I started my security career in early 2013, I discovered Bitcoin and cryptocurrency. When I wasn't working in the Microsoft Cyber Defense Operations Center (CDOC), I was hustling around the city installing Antminer ASIC miners for fellow bitcoin-curious miners.

Security and crypto have been overlapping pursuits since the beginning. As I lost funds in exchanges, I took self-custody very seriously. Fast forward to 2020, and at this time, I transitioned to work in Cyber Risk for a top hedge fund (Two Sigma) by day, teaching a DeFi class and degen-ing hard by night. Until one day, the BadgerDAO front end got hacked. I had some losses, but it got me thinking, "If someone who has been in Security for years can sign a malicious transaction without realizing it, and I'm watching all these crypto devs get hacked, then the non-tech folks coming into NFTs have no chance."

I gathered a bunch of security nerds, left the corporate world, and Boring Security was born. We pride ourselves on providing non-technical folks the tools they need to operate securely in this space (as well as other things like Flashbots recoveries and Discord Audits). Whether it's understanding Etherscan, Sleuthing, Solana architecture, or Multisig wallets, we create resources for folks to take on the world of self-custody from all aspects.

Thanks to this, I have created systems to prevent our alumni and me from making costly mistakes while signing (no costly signature mistakes since!). I strive to stay current in EVM Security, explain it to Boring Security students, and ensure they understand it. Also, being surrounded by the legends of the Security Council will only add fuel to the fire, so it synergizes well with my current mission and activities.

skills.cyberSecurity: 13 years in Security. System Administration for SIPRNet (3 years), Security Operations Center (1 year), Cloud Security Analyst (3 years), Cyber Security Auditor (4 years), and Founder of Boring Security (2 years)

The Security Council furthers our mission to maximize TVL secured. It is an extension of our commitment to the Arbitrum Foundation, ecosystem projects, and public service.
0xa0683d725420e2F75415806352Cd9c3fE10Fa9.experience:
We’re a founding member of the Security Alliance (SEAL) led by samczsun, an industry effort to raise the bar for blockchain security with a 24/7 emergency hotline for help with incident response, vulnerability disclosure, and other security concerns. More details here: https://securityalliance.org/

We have audited and/or have on retainer several leading protocols on Arbitrum, including Hyperliquid, Timeless, Perennial Finance, Y2K, and Premia. 

We work with some of the largest L1s—Solana Foundation, Aptos Labs, and Mysten Labs—and L2s—StarkNet, Scroll, and Mantle—to identify bugs in networks, application layers, custom precompiles, and more. Earlier this year, we discovered a critical vulnerability in Move’s bytecode verifier that put billions of dollars at risk across multiple chains. 

Prior to Zellic, we founded the #1 CTF (competitive hacking) team worldwide in 2020 and 2021. Our engineers bring a rich set of skills and backgrounds, including cryptography, web security, mobile security, low-level exploitation, and finance.

skills.cyberSecurity:
We have been a blockchain security firm since 2021, and have worked with 150+ clients on security reviews, advisory, and incident response. 

With regards to off-chain security, we have expertise spanning the major web application tech stacks — ranging from dynamic backends built in Javascript, Python, and Ruby, to high-performance APIs in Golang and Rust, to client-side applications in React and Electron, and cloud enclave stacks like KMS and AWS Nitro Enclaves.

As part of our security researchers’ bug bounty efforts, we have discovered vulnerabilities in the largest technology companies and public institutions including the US Department of Defense, Github, Yahoo, Shopify, PayPal, Adobe, CrowdStrike, Amazon, and Bitfinex.

I am Ryón Nixon, an attorney, entrepreneur, and investor deeply involved in the digital asset industry. I am from New York City, and earned my undergraduate and law degrees from the University of California, Los Angeles (“UCLA”). My legal experience includes a federal clerkship in the Central District of California, working as an associate in Brubaker Law’s venture capital practice group, where I collaborated with some of Silicon Valley's top startups.

 I started my law firm, Horizons Law & Consulting Group, in 2016 to focus my practice on the digital asset industry as an external general counsel. One of my first crypto clients was MyEtherWallet, the first non-custodial wallet on Ethereum. I counseled MyEtherWallet through corporate and transitional matters and its eventual acquisition by Consensys. I acted as Solana's external General Counsel from its genesis in 2018 through 2021, and I guided them through a highly successful token launch. As I grew my practice, I have worked with other major projects like Synthetix and Akash Network and large crypto funds, like Asymmetric. Beyond legal work, I have been involved in shaping industry-wide policies as a founding member of the Proof of Stake Alliance.

I pride myself on being more than just an attorney. I provide comprehensive commercial advice to my clients in addition to legal advice. As an entrepreneur myself, I am a co-founder of ParagonsDAO, an on-chain gaming guild protocol built from the Parallel ecosystem, and co-founder of Horizons Global and Mugen DAO Advisors, two well-regarded digital asset services providers based in the Cayman Islands.

I also operate Marin Digital Ventures, a family office fund that has invested in over 150 digital asset projects. With my holistic experience as an attorney, co-founder, and investor, my legal and commercial advice reflects my understanding of the perspectives of both projects and investors.

0xC6820fFD46a3D8045fD28c9f340a9011307f02.name: Jordan
0xC6820fFD46a3D8045fD28c9f340a9011307f02.title: Event Horizon | Founder
0xC6820fFD46a3D8045fD28c9f340a9011307f02.motivation:
TL;DR I've been publicly involved in the blockchain space since 2014 and have been personally motivated to improve and expand governance solutions for several years now. In particular, I have been actively championing citizen enfranchisement across the space. Through this endeavor, the Arbitrum ecosystem has become a home base for both myself and my team at Event Horizon, a public-good metagovernance layer.
--
One of several landing points of this motivation to build a more robust decentralized governance ecosystem was the creation of the novel delegation mechanism, Implicit Delegation (more info: https://medium.com/hvax/implicit-delegation-event-horizons-governance-solution-3815d61ded12). 

ID is a governance model emergent at the metagovernance level, which offers direct reward in the form of greater voice to retail voters who have the will to participate in governance. 

From a DAO perspective, ID brings the many great ideas and voices from the long-tail distribution of low-capital community members to the surface. I like to say that the best ideas in an ecosystem are often held in the minds of individuals with $50 in governance voice, so we need a better platform for these ideas to be heard.
0xC6820fFD46a3D8045fD28c9f340a9011307f02.experience:
The concept of Implicit delegation above has been actionized by my team at Event Horizon (https://EventHorizon.vote). 

Event Horizon has already mobilized >$20,000,000 of voting power across ~200 proposals on over 6 DAOs all on behalf of almost 200 retail voters at no cost to the user. This is $20,000,000 of voice amplification for the low-capital, high-conviction citizen which would never have been possible prior to Event Horizon's product.

However, beyond implicit delegation, myself and the broader Event Horizon team, are committed to evolving and protecting DAO ecosystems even beyond the scope of our core product.

This Security Council Candidacy is a continuation of this commitment.
